coredata.dc:title: Simple approach for the preparation of nitrogen and sulfur codoped carbon dots/reduced graphene oxide as host for high-rate lithiumsulfur batteries
coredata.dc:creator: Chabu, Johnny Muya
Zeng, Ke
Jin, Gaoyao
Zhang, Mengyuan
Li, Yajuan
Liu, You-Nian
coredata.dc:description: Exploring scalable and cost-effective material preparation route is critical for achieving the commercialization of lithium–sulfur batteries. Herein, nitrogen and sulfur codoped carbon dots/reduced graphene oxide (N,S-CDs/rGO) composites are facilely fabricated via hydrothermal and annealing treatment of a mixture of graphene oxide and egg-white without additional reactants. The egg-white, not only act as heteroatoms source, but also produce carbon dots. The as-prepared material possesses a porous architecture with N,S-codoping into both graphene and carbon dots. Benefiting from combined advantages of each constituent, such as superior electrical conductivity, abundant edges and doping sites, N,S-CDs/rGO cathodes (with 65 wt% and 76 wt% sulfur content) exhibits enhanced performances characterize by a higher rate capability and high sulfur utilization. For instance, N,S-CDs/rGO@S65 cathode exhibits a capacity of 1550 mAh g−1 at 0.5 C and still kept high capacities of 1038, 861.1 and 783.6 mAh g−1 at 1, 3, and 5 C respectively. Even at an ultra high rate of 7 C, a capacity of 740.8 mAh g−1 is sustained. Moreover the cathode retains 427.9 mAh g−1 reversible capacity after 600 cycles at 5 C. This work provides a new route toward the simple design of cathode for advanced Li–S cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium–sulfur battery
Green synthesis
Heteroatom doping
Egg-white proteins
Graphene oxide

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM and (b) TEM images of N,S-CDs/rGO (EDS inset); (c) selected area from which the elemental maps of (d) carbon, (e) nitrogen and (f) sulfur were obtained; (g) HRTEM of N,S-CDs/rGO (CDs are encircled in red). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(a) XPS surveys of N,S-CDs/rGO and GO, high resolution XPS of: C 1s of N,S-CDs/rGO and GO (b), S 2p (c) and N 1s (d) spectra of N,S-CDs/rGO.

(a) Raman spectra of N,S-CDs/rGO and GO; (b) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and the pore size distribution of N,S-CDs/rGO; (c) TGA curves of N,S-CDs/rGO@S76 and N,S-CDs/rGO@S65; (d) XRD patterns of elemental sulfur, N,S-CDs/rGO, N,S-CDs/rGO@S76 and N,S-CDs/rGO@S65.

(a) CV curves of N,S-CDs/rGO@S76 at a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1 for three consecutive cycle between 1.5 and 3 V; (b) the galvanostatic charge/discharge potential profiles for the first cycle at 0.5C; (c) discharge capacities at various rate capabilities of 0.5, 1, 3, 5 and 7 C; (d) Nyquist plots of N,S-CDs/rGO@S76 cathode before and after cycling at 0.5C; and (e) cycling performances of N,S-CDs/rGO@S76 at 0.5C and 3 C.

(a) Rate performances of N,S-CDs/rGO@S65 and rGO@S65; (b) the charge/discharge curves of N,S-CDs/rGO@S65 at 0.5, 1, 3, 5 and 7C; and (c) long-term cycling performance of N,S-CDs/rGO@S65 at high rate of 5C for 600 cycles.
Schematic illustration of the fabrication of the N,S-CDs/rGO and N,S-CDs/rGO@S composites.

head.ce:title: Simple approach for the preparation of nitrogen and sulfur codoped carbon dots/reduced graphene oxide as host for high-rate lithiumsulfur batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha, Hunan 410083, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Power Sources, Central South University, Changsha, Hunan 410083, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Power Sources

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Lubumbashi, Lubumbashi, BP, 1825, Congo D. R

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha, Hunan 410083, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Power Sources, Central South University, Changsha, Hunan 410083, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Power Sources

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Exploring scalable and cost-effective material preparation route is critical for achieving the commercialization of lithium–sulfur batteries. Herein, nitrogen and sulfur codoped carbon dots/reduced graphene oxide (N,S-CDs/rGO) composites are facilely fabricated via hydrothermal and annealing treatment of a mixture of graphene oxide and egg-white without additional reactants. The egg-white, not only act as heteroatoms source, but also produce carbon dots. The as-prepared material possesses a porous architecture with N,S-codoping into both graphene and carbon dots. Benefiting from combined advantages of each constituent, such as superior electrical conductivity, abundant edges and doping sites, N,S-CDs/rGO cathodes (with 65 wt% and 76 wt% sulfur content) exhibits enhanced performances characterize by a higher rate capability and high sulfur utilization. For instance, N,S-CDs/rGO@S65 cathode exhibits a capacity of 1550 mAh g−1 at 0.5 C and still kept high capacities of 1038, 861.1 and 783.6 mAh g−1 at 1, 3, and 5 C respectively. Even at an ultra high rate of 7 C, a capacity of 740.8 mAh g−1 is sustained. Moreover the cathode retains 427.9 mAh g−1 reversible capacity after 600 cycles at 5 C. This work provides a new route toward the simple design of cathode for advanced Li–S cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Designing novel energy-storage systems with high storage capability and efficiency represents an imperative for a sustainable energy future [1]. As a new type of power source, lithium–sulfur (Li–S) batteries have drawn great attention in recent years due to their unprecedented potential in meeting the future needs for high energy density and high power [2,3]. In addition, sulfur is widely available, non-toxic and extremely cheap. Li–S battery has received extensive research interest due to its higher specific capacity of 1675 mAh g−1, and a high energy density (2600 W h kg−1), which surpass existing state-of-the-art Li-ions batteries. Unfortunately, the lithium polysulfides (Li2Sx, 3 ≤ x ≤ 8) dissolution is the main unsolved issue, which leads to low sulfur utilization, fast capacity decay and low coulombic efficiency [4]. Currently, various strategies, such as designing new sulfur host materials [5–7], customizing novel electrolyte [8], altering the separator [9] and using interlayers [10], have been proposed to promote the electrochemical performance of Li–S cathode. Recently, introducing heteroatoms into carbon backbones with suitable porous structures has offered a great opportunity to further suppress the shuttling of soluble polysulfides [11–13]. In fact, heteroatoms can modify the electron density distribution of the host, which leads to high electronic conductivity and a stronger polysulfides binding capability. Especially, N-doped porous carbon materials have been extensively reported, owing to their significant elevated chemisorptions toward polysulfides and the architectural benefit [14,15]. Moreover, combining different heteroatoms would confer to the carbon nanostructure an even greater lithium polysulfides binding ability [16]. For instance, Pang et al. fabricated an ordered nanoporous carbon dual-doped with nitrogen and sulfur [17]. The cathode exhibited an extremely low capacity fading of 0.052% per cycle over 1100 cycles due to its strong interaction with polar polysulfides and improved electronic conductivity.

Generally, incorporating heteroatoms into the carbon scaffold often requires sophisticated steps and the use of harmful and toxic reagents which hinder their practicability for large scale application [18]. In this regard, it is essential to develop simple and safe methods for the mass production of doped cathode material. Recently, zero-dimensional carbon dots (CDs), reputed for their excellent photoluminescence properties, have emerged as potential heteroatom source [19]. Meanwhile, many pioneering works have demonstrated the possibility of using protein-rich biomasses under hydrothermal treatment as a way of sustainably producing heteroatom doped carbon dots [20–23].

Herein, egg-white (EW) is used as a precursor in the presence of graphene oxide to prepare N and S codoped carbon dots decorated graphene composites (N,S-CDs/rGO). When it was used as a cathode, the as-prepared N,S-CDs/rGO demonstrates excellent electrochemical properties.

Graphene oxide was prepared following a modified Hummers method [24]. Egg-white from chicken eggs was freeze-dried and grinded to fine powder. Next, the egg-white powder (2 g) was added to a 35 mL GO (4 mg mL−1) aqueous solution. The mixture was ultrasonically treated for 1 h then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon container; and disposed into a stainless steel autoclave. The autoclave was placed into a preheated oven and kept at 250 °C for 12 h. Afterward, the resulting black monolith was dried and placed into a tube furnace (OTF-1200X–III–S, Kejing Technology Co., Ltd. Hefei, China) and annealed at 800 °C for 2 h under N2 gas flow at a rate of 10 °C min−1. Following, the material was dispersed into diluted HCl solution and stirred overnight to remove contaminants. Subsequently, it was collected using vacuum filtration and washed repeatedly with water until pH reached 7. Finally, the product N,S-CDs/rGO was dried and grinded with sulfur and placed in the tube furnace at 155 °C under N2 to load sulfur. The rGO@S sample was obtained following identical protocol except no egg-white was added.

SEM images and SEM maps were obtained by a scanning electron microscope (FEI HELIOS NanoLab 600i, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained from Titan G2 60-300 (FEI, USA), which includes corrector technologies enabling resolution of 80 pm. Powder X-ray diffractometer (XRD) patterns were collected on a D/max 2550 (Rigaku, Japan) using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.1541 nm) starting from 10° to 80° (2θ). Pore structure was studied by N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms via a surface area and porosity analyser (ASAP 2020, Micromeritics Inc., USA). The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area was calculated according to the BET model, while the pore size distribution (PSD) was determined by the nonlocal density functional theory (NL-DFT) method. X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS) was carried out with an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher-VG Scientific, USA). Raman spectra were obtained using reflex Raman system (Renishaw plc., Wotton-under-Edge, UK) equipped with a microscope under 785 nm laser radiation at 1.5 mW. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was executed using a TGA (SDT Q600 v8.0, USA) in Ar atmosphere at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1. Particles sizes were measured using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano-ZS instrument (ZEN3600, Malvern Instruments, UK).

Measurements for all cathodes were evaluated on 2025 coin-type cells. The cathodes were fabricated by mixing 70 wt% active materials, 20 wt% acetylene black and 10 wt% PVDF in NMP and vigorously stirred for 48 h to form a slurry. Following, the slurry was casted onto an Al foil and dried at 50 °C for 24 h in a vacuum oven. The electrochemical behaviors of the cathodes with different areal mass loading of the active materials (1.5, 0.97 and 0.85 mg cm−2) were investigated. The cells were assembled in an argon-filled glove box (MBraun, Germany). Lithium metal was used as the anode and Celgard 2300 as separator. The electrolyte was 1 M LiTFSI in a mixed solvent of DOL/DME (1: 1 V/V) with 1.0% LiNO3. The volume of electrolyte in each cell was 100 μL. Galvanostatic charge and discharge were carried out using a LAND CT2001A Battery Test System (Jinnuo Electronic Co., Wuhan, China) under the potential window 1.5 and 2.8 V (vs. Li/Li+). The calculation of specific capacity was based on the mass of sulfur. Cyclic voltammograms (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests were performed on a CHI 650D electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Instruments Co., Shanghai, China). CV tests were using potential range within 1.5–3 V (vs. Li/Li+) at a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1 and EIS analysis was performed using AC signal of 5 mV and frequency ranges from 100 kHz to 10 mHz.

The main constituent of chicken egg-white (EW) is ovalbumin (ca. 54% of the total egg-white proteins). As depicted in Scheme 1 , upon mixing egg-white with GO, ovalbumin and other components are spontaneously adsorbed on the surface of GO nanosheets. This immobilization of EW on GO nanosheets is the result of several interactions, such as hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic interaction, electrostatic interaction and π-π stacking [25]. The possible formation mechanism of CDs from egg-white has been proposed elsewhere [23]. It is assumed that during the hydrothermal process, the egg-white undergoes hydrolysis, polymerization and dehydration, eventually can be carbonized into carbon dots (CDs). Meanwhile, GO with its open 2D high surface area acts as a template interacting with CDs. By virtue of π–π stacking interactions, and experimental conditions, GO is reduced and self-assemble into a 3D porous structure [22].

Specifically, in the initial stage of the reaction, hydrolyzed egg-white products, which include amino acids and peptides, are expected to bind themselves onto the GO sheets before undergoing partial polymerization with time. And in the final stage of the hydrothermal process, they are partially carbonized into N,S-codoped CDs [26–28]. It is known that amino acids not only act as reductants, but also provide dopants, thus doping of graphene occurred during the process [26]. Subsequent annealing completes the carbonization of the remained unbound egg-white components. This synthetic route considerably minimizes the number of preparation steps without employing either unstable precursors or excessive harmful reagents; particularly, no-toxic by-products are generated. More importantly, egg-white is commercially available, eco-friendly and cost-effective. As shown in Fig. 1 a and b, SEM and TEM images of the as-prepared N,S-CDs/rGO composites reveal a three-dimensional porous structure. From the SEM image (Fig. 1a), crumpled sheets are forming a tightly compact solid, implying a severe aggregation of graphene sheets occurred.

The EDX analysis (inset Fig. 1b) of N,S-CDs/rGO reveals the existence of C, N, O and S atoms within the material with atomic contents of 2.9% and 3.8% for N and S, respectively. From the selected area of the N,S-CDs/rGO (Fig. 1c), SEM elemental mapping further attest that C, N and S atoms are homogeneously distributed within the material (Fig. 1d–f), in full accord with EDX analysis. The high resolution TEM image reveals that tiny carbon dots (encircled in red) are anchored on the surface of graphene (Fig. 1g), which is supported by the lattice spacing of 0.21 and 0.34 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal face of graphite and graphitic crystallites of N,S-CDs respectively [29]. To further confirm the formation of CDs, we carried out the TEM analysis of the yellow suspension collected after hydrothermal treatment. As shown in Fig. S1a, aggregated CDs are clearly observed and EDX also reveals the existence of N and S atoms, implying that doping has successfully occurred in CDs. Therefore, if N and S are found both in graphene and CDs, the interaction for Li+ and polysulfides anions would be synchronously promoted. Obviously, upon exposing the suspension to a UV beam (365 nm), a blue luminescence attributed to the existence of CDs can be seen (Fig. S1b). Their sizes were measured between 0.6 and 1.8 nm (Fig. S1c). These results are fully consistent with previous reports [19,30]. Additional SEM analysis after loading sulfur is depicted in Fig. S2, and it can be clearly identified elemental bulk sulfur over graphene sheets.

XPS spectrum of N,S-CDs/rGO is depicted in Fig. 2 a, and the peaks at 165.9, 225.6, 286.6, 399.5 and 532.5 eV are assigned to S 2p, S 2s, C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively. Obviously, neither N peak nor S peak is present in GO, confirming the successful heteroatom doping initiated by egg-white. As listed in Table S1, the content of N and S elements in N,S-CDs/rGO before (after) annealing is 7.66 at.% (2.77 at.%) and 0.89 at.% (3.55 at.%), respectively. It is found that the N and S contents in the materials prior to annealing are almost identical to the EDX elemental contents of CDs. Therefore, the high N content in the product before annealing is mostly attributable to the adsorbed CDs and remaining egg-white components which are partially removed during the annealing process. Interestingly, after the annealing treatment, sulfur atomic content increases, testifying the formation of new bonds as a result of the molecular disintegration of remained egg-white components and in situ generation of S-bearing species which react with functional groups of rGO. Above hypothesis is confirmed by the considerable differences in the N and S bond status before and after annealing (see Fig. S3, Fig. 2 c & d). The unprecedented doping efficiency of the method allows us to achieve relatively high N and S contents and require fewer steps compared to previous report [30–33]. The decreased O 1s peak in N,S-CDs/rGO XPS survey expresses the partial removal of functional groups. This is also evidenced in the high resolution C 1s XPS spectra compared to the one of GO, which displays a drastic change in the intensity of peaks at 286.6 eV (CO), 288.5 eV (OCO); and an increase of CC/CC peak at 284.7 eV and the formation of CN/CS peaks at 285.8 eV (Fig. 2b) [34]. However, there are residual oxygenated functional groups which can capture lithium polysulfides through surface adsorption, thus promoting the battery performance [35]. As illustrated in Fig. 2c, the S 2p peak is deconvoluted into two distinctive peaks at 163.9 eV (CS) and 165.1 eV (CSC) belonging to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 respectively, which correspond to thiophenic sulfur [24]. Oxidized sulfur (SOx) peak at 167.9 eV is also observed. The observed peaks and their positions are similar for the unannealed sample except that the peak related to SH moieties (162 eV) was not detected after annealing and the intensity of peaks assigned to thiophenic sulfur largely evolves and increases exponentially (Fig. S3a). The high resolution N 1s XPS spectra after annealing is also different from the one of the unannealed sample depicted in Fig. S3b. As shown in Fig. 2d, the N 1s peak can be decomposed into four peaks located at 398.5, 399.9, 401.0 and 403.2 eV, which are assigned to pyridinic, pyrrolic, graphitic and oxidized nitrogen, respectively. In the unannealed sample, pyrrolic-N and graphitic-N are dominant moieties and no peak related to oxidized-N was found (Fig. S3b). It is worth noting that the energy position of graphitic-N is similar to that of the NH groups in CDs. And Pyrrolic-N and NH groups’ peaks have been reported as main nitrogen species for CDs obtained by hydrothermal treatment of egg-white [23]. This confirms that CDs are main contributor of the N content in the unannealed sample. Pyridinic N-dopants, among all other configurations are reported to strongly restrain the soluble lithium polysulfides [36].

Subsequently, Raman spectra of N,S-CDs/rGO and GO were applied to elucidate the structure information. As shown in Fig. 3 a, all samples exhibit distinct peaks at 1347 and 1596 cm−1 related to D and G band, respectively. Notably, there is a small red shift of approximately 38 cm−1 of the peaks in N,S-CDs/rGO, possibly caused by the doping and loading of CDs on graphene sheets. Moreover, the intensity ratio ID/IG of N,S-CDs/rGO is 1.33, much higher than that of GO (0.74), suggesting the introduction of defects and vacancies is most likely induced by the N, S codoping and CDs.

BET specific surface area of N,S-CDs/rGO is low (61.5 m2 g−1) with 0.091 cm3g–1 pore volume, strongly indicating the stacking of graphene sheets. We presumed that such low surface area and pore volume is acceptable given the merits of the 3D graphene structure and would be largely compensated by the N, S co-doping to minimize the shuttle phenomenon. Apparently, N,S-CDs/rGO composite shows a combination of type II and type IV isotherm with H4 hysteresis loop (Fig. 3b) [37]. Moreover the pore size distribution displays all three kinds of pores, ranging from micropores to macropores (inset Fig. 3b). Such architecture would confer N,S-CDs/rGO cathode excellent performance given the role of each pores [38]. Next, electrodes were prepared after loading sulfur, designated as N,S-CDs/rGO@S76 and N,S-CDs/rGO@S65 with 76 wt% and 65 wt% S content, respectively (Fig. 3c). For comparison, rGO@S65 with 65 wt% sulfur content was prepared. Following, XRD measurement was executed for elemental sulfur, N,S-CDs/rGO, N,S-CDs/rGO@S76 and N,S-CDs/rGO@S65 composites. As illustrated in Fig. 3d, a strong peak (002) related to the graphitic peak of carbon at 25.7° and a weak peak (101) at 43.4° affiliated to defects in the carbon phase are clearly visible for N,S-CDs/rGO, but slightly weakened in N,S-CDs/rGO@S65, then disappeared in N,S-CDs/rGO@S76 due to high S content. Additionally, the sulfur orthorhombic crystalline phase is retained after being loaded in the composite. To electrochemically assess the properties of the N,S-CDs/rGO@S65 cathode, CV was carried out within potential range of 1.5–3 V at a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the CVs display two typical reduction peaks at 2.0 and 2.3 V related to the multistep conversion of S8 with lithium and one oxidation peak at 2.43 V. The curves in the following cycles are almost overlapped, and the oxidation peak shifts to 2.40 V, expressing low polarization of the electrode and good reversibility [39]. The first charge/discharge voltage profiles of N,S-CDs/rGO@S76 at 0.5 C exhibit two typical plateaus, in accordance with the CV result (Fig. 4b). A high initial discharge capacity of 1397 mAh g−1 is achieved, which attests of the high sulfur utilization. Furthermore, the flat second plateau suggests a minor kinetic barrier [40]. This can be attributed to the synergetic effect of the advantageous pore structure and simultaneous N,S-codoping both in graphene and CDs, providing a strong chemisorptions and physical barrier against the diffusion of lithium polysulfides. The N,S-CDs/rGO@S76 electrode with 0.85 mg cm−2 areal sulfur loading shows excellent rate capability, high sulfur utilization and relatively high specific capacities of 1397, 956.1, 749.7, 675.3 and 619.2 mAh g−1 at 0.5, 1, 3, 5, and 7 C, respectively (Fig. 4c). Once the current density went back to 0.5 C, the cathode kept a high reversible capacity of 813.5 mAh g−1.

The EIS measurements of N,S-CDs/rGO@S76 is depicted in Fig. 4d, and a depressed semicircle related to the charge transfer resistance (electrode/electrolyte interface) in the high frequency region and an inclined line referred to as the Warburg impedance is visible [41]. However, it can be seen that the cathode exhibit a lower resistance after 90 cycles compared to the fresh cell, which could be explained by the gradual penetration of the electrolyte in the cathode structures, thus reducing the charge transfer resistance [42]. At current densities of 0.5 and 3C for 150 and 200 cycles, N,S-CDs/rGO@S76 portrays excellent stability with high initial capacities of 1296 and 872 mAh g−1, respectively (Fig. 4e). Notably, fast capacity decay was observed at low current density of 0.5 C, as a result of the dissolution of the intermediates polysulfides into the electrolyte causing the loss of the active materials. Moreover it has been reported that the retention time of high-ordered lithium polysulfides in the electrolyte at low C rate is much longer compared to higher current densities [43]. This is verified by the stable cycling stability at high current rate.

The rate performances of N,S-CDs/rGO@S65 and rGO@S65 cathodes with identical areal sulfur loading of 0.97 mg cm−2 from 0.5, 1, 3, 5 and 7 C are illustrated in Fig. 5 a. Exceptionally, the N,S-CDs/rGO@S65 cathode exhibits a capacity of 1550 mAh g−1 at 0.5 C, which corresponds to 93% sulfur utilization and still keeps high capacities of 1038, 861.1 and 783.6 mAh g−1 at 1, 3, and 5 C, respectively, far beyond that of rGO@S65. Even after increasing the rate to 7 C, N,S-CDs/rGO@65 retains a high reversible capacity of 740.8 mAh g−1. As the current rate increases from 3 to 7 C, only a small decrease of the discharge capacity is recorded. In addition, when the rate is restored back to 0.5 C, a reversible capacity of 909.5 mAh g−1 is recovered. Furthermore, the discharge/charge voltage profiles at selected C rates for the first cycles are shown in Fig. 5b. Remarkably, all discharge curves exhibit two distinctive plateaus, implying an enhanced high-rate capability compared to rGO@S65 (Fig. S4).

Long-term cycling of N,S-CDs/rGO@S65 (0.85 mg cm−2 areal sulfur loading) was executed at 5 C for 600 cycles (Fig. 5c), the electrode delivers 783.7 mAh g−1 initial discharge capacity and retains 427.9 mAh g−1. Particularly, the coulombic efficiency is kept above 95%. However, when the areal sulfur loading is increased to 1.5 mg cm−2, the N,S-CDs/rGO@65 cathode was tested at 3C for 350 cycles (Fig. S5), and still it demonstrated high initial discharge capacity of 833.8 mAh g−1 and retains 409.3 mAh g−1. In view of the compositional and architectural advantages of the N,S-CDs/rGO hybrids, their excellent overall electrochemical performances can be attributed to the strong polysulfides binding stimulated by the synchronous chemisorptions from N,S-codoped graphene and CDs. In fact, the codoping in both components provides more adsorption sites which prevents the release of polysulfides, but also improves the conductivity. Meanwhile, the porous configuration allows excellent ions and electron transportation.

In summary, a simple and scalable strategy toward a proteins self-organized nitrogen and sulfur doped carbon dots/graphene is presented. Benefiting from the synchronous N and S enriched surface both in graphene and embedded carbon dots, which together provides many adsorption sites for polysulfides, the N,S-CDs/rGO based cathodes (N,S-CDs/rGO@S76 and N,S-CDs/rGO@S65) deliver superior performances characterize by a higher rate capability and high sulfur utilization. N,S-CDs/rGO@S65 cathode exhibits a capacity of 1550 mAh g−1 at 0.5 C and still keeps high capacities of 1038, 861.1 and 783.6 mAh g−1 at 1, 3, and 5 C respectively, much higher than rGO@S65. Even after increasing the rate to 7 C, the N,S-CDs/rGO@65 electrode still retains a high reversible capacity of 740.8 mAh g−1. Notably, long-term cycling at 5 C for 600 cycles, the cathode retains 427.9 mAh g−1. This work opens up new perspective toward the design of cost-affordable codoped cathode for Li–S batteries.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21676304 and 21636010).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matchemphys.2019.03.019.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sustainability and in situ monitoring in battery development
More reliable lithium–sulfur batteries: status, solutions and prospects
Lithium–sulfur batteries: electrochemistry, materials, and prospects
Revisiting the role of polysulfides in lithium–sulfur batteries
Soft template synthesis of acetylene black/manganese dioxide nanosheets composites as efficient sulfur hosts for lithium–sulfur batteries
Lithium battery using sulfur infiltrated in three-dimensional flower-like hierarchical porous carbon electrode
Porous carbon derived from rice husks as sustainable bioresources: insights into the role of micro-/mesoporous hierarchy in hosting active species for lithium–sulphur batteries
Tailored Li2S–P2S5 glass-ceramic electrolyte by MoS2 doping, possessing high ionic conductivity for all-solid-state lithium–sulfur batteries
Effective trapping of lithium polysulfides using a functionalized carbon nanotube-coated separator for lithium–sulfur cells with enhanced cycling stability
Suppressing self-discharge and shuttle effect of lithium–sulfur batteries with V2O5-decorated carbon nanofiber interlayer
Design principles for heteroatom-doped nanocarbon to achieve strong snchoring of polysulfides for lithium–sulfur batteries
Freestanding and sandwich-structured electrode material with high areal mass loading for long-life lithium–sulfur batteries
Atomic layer deposited TiO2 on a nitrogen-doped graphene/sulfur electrode for high performance lithium–sulfur batteries
Strong lithium polysulfide chemisorption on electroactive sites of nitrogen‐doped carbon composites for high‐performance lithium–sulfur battery cathodes
Hydroxylated N-doped carbon nanotube-sulfur composites as cathodes for high-performance lithium–sulfur batteries
Hierarchical 3D nitrogen and phosphorous codoped graphene/carbon nanotubes–sulfur composite with synergistic effect for high performance of lithium–sulfur batteries
A nitrogen and sulfur dual‐doped carbon derived from Polyrhodanine@Cellulose for advanced lithium–sulfur batteries
One-pot synthesis of nitrogen-rich carbon dots decorated graphene oxide as metal-free electrocatalyst for oxygen reduction reaction
One-step, green, and economic synthesis of water-soluble photoluminescent carbon dots by hydrothermal treatment of wheat straw, and their bio-applications in labeling, imaging, and sensing
Carbon quantum dots hydrothermally synthesized from chitin
